summary: Jeff Atwood critiques Microsoft's MSDN documentation for containing vast amounts of 'undocumentation' - help entries that look like documentation but provide no useful information beyond what the method signature already tells you. He cites Scott Swigart's observation that Microsoft spent considerable resources creating, proofreading, and publishing these useless entries. The post highlights examples ranging from completely empty documentation to code samples that are either trivially useless or factually incorrect. Atwood warns against tools like GhostDoc that can produce similar undocumentation automatically, urging developers to either write genuinely helpful documentation or honestly acknowledge when documentation doesn't exist. He concludes by suggesting that making .NET framework documentation a wiki might help solve the problem.
key_points: Undocumentation is content that has the appearance of documentation but provides no information beyond what's already obvious from the code itself
Microsoft invested significant resources creating, proofreading, and publishing documentation that tells users nothing they couldn't infer from method signatures
Tools like GhostDoc can automate the production of useless undocumentation, making it easy to create the appearance of documentation without the substance
Even when MSDN includes code samples, they can be either trivially useless one-liners or contain factual errors like incorrect Fahrenheit-to-Celsius conversion formulas
The solution isn't to pretend documentation exists - it's to either write real documentation or honestly mark things as undocumented
money_quotes: This looks like documentation. It shows up in the help alongside documentation, it's indexed like documentation, but it's not documentation.
I don't know how much time was spent undocumenting things, but I'm guessing that if you add it all up, it's a lot.
Can we stop pretending? Can you just mark everything as Undocumented until you get around to writing real documentation for it?
For a simple test, if it doesn't include a code example, it's not documented.
Please don't knowingly produce undocumentation for your applications. Have some respect for your users and your fellow developers.
Either take the time to write helpful documentation, or have the guts to acknowledge that there simply is no documentation.
I guess C++ developers really are smarter.
Why not make the .NET framework documentation a Wiki?
key_insight: Documentation that merely restates what code already shows is worse than no documentation at all, because it wastes both the writer's and reader's time while creating the false appearance of helpfulness.
